sentence: Subjective:
The patient is a 35-year-old male who presented to the office with a right knee injury sustained during a soccer game three days ago.
He reports a sudden pop in his knee and describes the sensation as feeling like his knee is not moving as well as it used to, with limited function.
The patient rates his pain as a 6-7 out of 10, which has decreased to 4-5 with the use of ibuprofen.
He denies any fever.
Past medical history:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications:
I prescribed ibuprofen for pain relief.
Objective:
Vital signs:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
The patient has a marked limp on the right leg upon ambulation.
There is significant effusion and a slight bit of ecchymosis in the right knee.
Palpation of the medial joint line elicits pain.
The patient has difficulty with passive range of motion, with lacking a few degrees of terminal extension to 115 degrees and lacking about 20-30 degrees of terminal flexion due to pain.
The Lachman exam is positive.
Test Results:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's right knee injury is suggestive of an anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tear.
Problem list: ACL tear; status: suspected.
Plan:
I ordered an MRI to evaluate the integrity of the ACL.
I prescribed meloxicam 50mg a day for anti-inflammatory purposes.
I advised the patient to ice the knee if needed, moderate their activities, wear a knee immobilizer, and use crutches to reduce stress and strain on the knee.
I also instructed the patient to limit their activities, including work activities, and to avoid jogging until the results of the MRI are available.
Follow-up: I will get back with the patient as soon as the results of the MRI are available.
The patient will be scheduled for an MRI by the nurse.
